segments.text: June 26, 2025. 931 AM, Eastern. The opening bell rings on NASDAQ and a stock with the ticker symbol
OST begins to die. 94% gone. In a single trading session, $950 million, not lost over months,
not over weeks, destroyed in hours. And the people who lost everything, they had been convinced to
buy by AI-generated deep-fake videos of Goldman Sachs executives who never said those words and
global celebrities who had never heard of this company. Based on federal court filings, this is
the most brazen AI-powered stock fraud in NASDAQ history, and the men who allegedly built it
never set foot on American soil. Likeway Sands name meant nothing on Wall Street before 2025.
He became co-CEO of Austin Technology Group in early 2025. A Cayman Islands registered company with
principal operations in Nanjing, China listed on NASDAQ, claiming to manufacture LCD display modules
for consumer electronics and automotive displays. Annual revenues of just $38 million, losses of
$10.6 million, institutional ownership, near zero. This was background noise, wallpaper,
the kind of micro-cap stock so boring and so ignored that nobody, not analysts, not journalists,
not regulators, was paying any attention. And that was the entire point. See, the best cons don't
start with flash. They start with boring because a stock nobody's watching is a stock nobody's
protecting. No analysts covering the books. No institutional money asking hard questions.
It's the financial equivalent of an empty house with an unlocked door. And according to the DOJ
indictment filed September 10, 2025 in Virginia's Eastern District, Laikuei Sen and his
co-conspirator, financial advisor Yan Zhao, also known as Hank Shee, Hank Shou, Altman, and Bob.
Didn't just walk through that door. They brought an army. Armed with something Wall Street had
never seen weaponized at this scale. Artificial intelligence. The scheme's groundwork was laid in
late 2024, but the first strike came in April 2025. Step one was almost elegant. On April 15,
they closed a $5 million registered direct offering, placing approximately 9 million shares
with at least 15 co-conspirators at just $0.55 each. But with warrants attached,
the real average cost per share worked out to roughly $0.19. 19.
Step two was Boulder. Between May 3 and May 7, a warrant exchange agreement converted over
$0.18 million in to nearly $71 million new shares at zero cost. No cash, no purchase.
Shares conjured from nothing dropped directly into the hands of 15 conspirators.
Here is the number that should make your stomach drop. After those two transactions,
approximately 80 million shares sitting in the hands of 15 people at an average cost of roughly
19 cents per share, a publicly traded NASDAQ company for pennies on the dollar.
And on May 11, 2025, the pump began. And this is where it gets truly terrifying.
This wasn't your typical pump and dump with sketchy anonymous accounts. This was sophisticated.
This was AI. According to the Civil Complaint filed February 16, 2026 in the Southern District of New York,
the promoters built fake social media profiles using names, photographs, and professional
credentials stolen from real, living, SEC registered, and Finra registered investment advisors.
Real people, real licenses, real headshots pulled from real LinkedIn profiles.
When investors Googled these advisors, they checked out. The names matched actual Finra
registrations on the official database. Everything looked legitimate because everything was real,
except the person posting wasn't the person in the photo. But they went even further.
AI generated deep fake videos, Goldman Sachs chief global market strategist David Costan,
recommending OST stock on video, except he never said those words. Elon Musk, Mark Zuckerberg,
appearing to endorse this company through paid Instagram and Facebook ads, except they had never
heard of it. Victims were funneled into WhatsApp groups designed to sound like legitimate financial
communities, including one literally named Karen Feinerman's free exchange group sharing,
using the identity of the well-known CNBC financial personality.
Inside those groups, promoters posing as fellow investors issued daily buying instructions.
Today we focus on OST by between $450 and $5. Hold. They promised 15 to 25% weekly returns.
A fake research report circulated with fabricated acquisition rumors and price targets.
And anyone who pushed back or asked a single hard question was immediately removed from the group.
But here's the most diabolical part of the whole setup. The same WhatsApp groups had previously
recommended other stocks that actually made small gains. Just enough to build real trust.
Just enough to make you go bigger next time. The stock moved from $0.78 on April 14th
to a peak of $9.40 on June 26th. An 1175% increase in 73 days from a $22 million
company to over a billion dollars in market cap. On deep fakes and WhatsApp messages.
And nobody, not Finra, not the SEC, not NASDAQ, caught it in time.
Now picture this. You're a retail investor, Ohio, Florida, Texas, or maybe Tel Aviv, Toronto,
Berlin. You've been in one of those WhatsApp groups for months. The previous tips made you real
money a few thousand dollars enough to trust the group enough to go bigger this time.
So you put in 20,000, maybe 50,000, maybe your retirement savings.
Because a Goldman Sachs strategist on video said this stock was going to $15.
June 26th, 2025. OST hits $9.40. It's peak. By 10am, $4.
By noon, $1.50. By close, $0.55. 94.1% gone in one day.
$950 million in market capitalization destroyed.
Individual victims across the United States, Israel, Canada, Germany, the Netherlands, Switzerland,
Italy, and the United Kingdom lost anywhere from $10,000 to over $800,000 each.
Hundreds of victims identified across multiple ongoing lawsuits.
And the 15 co-conspirators, according to federal prosecutors, they had been dumping their shares
the entire way up. While the WhatsApp groups screamed by and hold, the people running those groups
were selling everything. The DOJ indictment alleges the scheme netted over $110 million
in illicit proceeds for the defendants and their co-conspirators combined.
But here's the part that genuinely haunts me. The real financial advisors, the ones whose identities
were stolen, had absolutely no idea any of this was happening. They woke up to angry phone calls
from investors demanding explanations for recommendations they never made. Compliance officers pulling
their records, finra opening inquiries against innocent people. One victim advisor reportedly said,
I spent 20 years building my reputation and someone on the other side of the world destroyed it
with a computer. That is the new crime, your face, your name, your career, hijacked by artificial
intelligence and used to steal from strangers across the planet. Here's what happened next.
Finra's market abuse group flagged the anomaly. The trading patterns on OST were unlike anything
in their surveillance history. They referred it to the DOJ. The FBI and the SEC office of Inspector
General led the investigation. On September 12, 2025, the indictment was unsealed in Virginia's
eastern district. Like we send and y'all charged with conspiracy to commit securities fraud,
wire fraud, and substantive counts of both. NASDAQ immediately halted trading of OST shares,
indefinitely. The cases assigned to Judge Michael S. Nockmanoff in Alexandria, Virginia.
Both defendants are Chinese nationals. Neither was in the United States when the indictment dropped.
And as of this recording, neither has been arrested. Both are believed to be outside US jurisdiction.
That is the answer. That is how they got away with it. They never had to step foot in America to
rob American investors. The deep fakes did the talking. The stolen identities did the convincing.
The WhatsApp groups did the selling. And by the time the algorithms caught the pattern, the money
was already gone. The real victims aren't in court rooms. They're in living rooms. Retirement
accounts wiped. College funds gone. Life savings that took 20 years to build. Erased in six hours.
And here is what makes this case different from every pump and dump in history.
Previous scams used anonymous tips, bought accounts, and sketchy newsletters. You could feel the scam
if you were paying attention. This one? This one looked real. The advisor on video was a real person,
just not the one actually speaking. Their credentials checked out on FINRAZONE database.
Their face matched a verified LinkedIn profile. Deep fake technology removed the one thing
retail investors have always been able to rely on. The ability to tell the difference between a real
human and a fabricated one. And here is the warning nobody is saying loudly enough. Prosecutors say
the OST case was not an isolated experiment. Investigators have flagged a broader wave of AI-powered
market manipulation schemes in 2025 that collectively cost investors hundreds of millions of dollars.
This was a blueprint. And the tools they used are getting cheaper, faster, and more convincing
every single day. Right now, anyone with a laptop and the right software can put words in
Warren Buffett's mouth, can steal your face, and use it to rob a stranger on the other side of the
world. The question isn't whether this happens again. It's whether we catch it faster next time.
And if I'm being honest with you, I don't know the answer. But I know this. The first step is
knowing it's possible. And now you do. If this story changed the way you think about your next
investment, hit subscribe. We cover the story's nobody else is telling, real money, real victims,
real consequences. Drop a comment. Have you ever been targeted by a stock scam and what tipped you
off? I read every single one. Next week, the most Googled person in America and the murder case
that nobody saw coming.
